sentences.text: Yes.
As I was saying, my life was still a life of, relatively speaking, a privileged person, because my father was making a living.

And most of my friends' parents-- people who came from Warsaw were, of course, better off than the people who came from the surrounding little towns and villages who had been dispossessed overnight with whatever they could carry and brought to the Warsaw ghetto with very few possessions.
Life in the ghetto was a life of contrasts.
We have contrasts here between people who are poor and people who are very wealthy, but you have to bring it to a totally different level.
You were rich if you could eat and had a room to live in.
When you were poor, you were homeless.
You had nothing to eat.

Every morning when we came out into the street, we would see corpses lying on the-- on the ground in the streets, people who had died overnight from starvation and later on also from typhus, which was a big epidemic in the Warsaw ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lived-- the-- the clinic where we lived, the windows were on the street.
And you could see a corner, at which there was one of the official gates to the ghetto of Warsaw.
So from my window, I could look at the activities at this border crossing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was manned by both German-- I don't know, police, SS, gestapo, something like that-- and Poles.

And you could see groups of Jewish children in the morning trying to run through out of the ghetto, because they would go and try to beg, steal, buy some food and smuggled it back in the evening into the ghetto.
And sometimes, there would be some relatively kind people who would let them out or in.
And sometimes, there would be somebody cruel, who would try to grab them and beat them.
And they always tried to run in a pack, so at least some would get through.
And you could see groups of-- of workers, because some Jews worked outside of the ghetto legally.
And they would be marched out under guard.
And they too in the evening would come back and would be sometimes searched for whatever they were smuggling.
They always smuggled food back in to their family.
So there were people who were very badly off.
And at the same time, I know that there were-- there were nightclubs, several nightclubs.
Of course, there was a curfew, so if you went to a nightclub, you had to stay there all night.
And there was a theater.
There was a concert hall, so there was some cultural life.
There were-- there were underground newspapers being published, both in the ghetto and outside of the ghetto.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so there were-- as I-- as I'm telling you, there were great variations in how people lived.
I was actually in the ghetto probably about a year.
Until approximately June of 1942, it was a relatively quiet life for me there.
I remember, for example, that we were inoculated against [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] typhus.
And probably one of those shots cost several hundred dollars, because this was one of the big causes of death in the ghetto.
And it was, of course, privately done.
It was very rare.
It was hard to get.
And so I'm just telling you that to show you that my life whereas it wasn't, of course, as good as it had been before, I don't remember being hungry.
And I remember studying and still living a life of or more or less a young-- a young person, a-- a young teenager.
I remember that my girlfriends were still interested in music.
I mean, in-- we were just getting then swing from United States.
I remember they had some records that we played.
And we tried to learn how to dance and-- so it was still a semi-normal life.
The buildings were sort of organized to try to help the people who were really unfortunate.
There were what you would call soup kitchens practically in every apartment house.
And what we had, we did share with other people.
But there were still very big differences in the-- in the level of-- of quality of life of different people.
There was relatively little contact with the Germans, which is, of course, a big advantage of a ghetto.
It has many disadvantages.
And-- but you didn't constantly encounter the Germans, so you-- you-- I was never beaten, for example.
Incidentally in the whole war, I was never beaten.
I've seen it, of course.
But personally, just randomly, I was not beaten.
Just luck.
Just luck.
To have survived the war, you had to have luck many times and wits many times, you know, quick wits.
And I, of course, being a child, can only attribute the quick wits to my father primarily, which he did have, and to luck, you know.
Oh, God.
I can not carry a tune.
[LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Look, just pure statistics.
Out of 3 and 1/2 million of Jews in Poland, approximately 50,000 or so survived.
Obviously, that is not just quick wits.
That is a lot of luck.
It's a combination.
But you do have to have strengths.
Weak people did not survive.
Yes.
And again, all this change is very abrupt.

In June of 1942, the Germans announced that the majority of the population of the ghetto of Warsaw-- not all of it, but the majority-- would be transported to what they said were working camps in the east.

And at the beginning, we really believed that this would be working camps, so much so that in the first few days, volunteers showed up to be moved, because people who had a very-- very difficult life in Warsaw, who couldn't make a living, who couldn't eat, the Germans would say, well, we will feed you.
You will work.
We will find work for you.
We will feed you, and life will be better.
So for the first few days, there were actually volunteers.
At the beginning, the idea was that only the people who were not employed by the Germans and their families would be transported.
And in the ghetto by that time, there were factories set up by the Germans, brush factories and textile factories primarily, where the Jews were working.
And those who worked had official papers, and they were supposed to be allowed to stay.
Gradually, of course, more and more of those people lost their papers and were transported out of the ghetto of Warsaw.
My father was a doctor in one such factory.

And, in fact, the Jewish man who owned that factory or was the son of the owners had been educated in Germany in textile fabrication and design, and knew the German, by coincidence, who was assigned to take over that factory.
A man by the name of Schultz was the German.
And our friend, his name was Rowinski, R-O-W-I-N-S-K-I.
And my father was a doctor in that factory.
So from our apartment, he went every morning with his papers to the factory, worked there all day, came back in the evening.
Very soon after the-- we called it actions and later on selections started.

People were not willing to go-- to go willingly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] partly because they-- they were unsure of what happened there, and partly because the news started to filter back that there was something wrong with this whole project.
The trains which were supposed to go deep into Russia, the same trains were coming back in the evening of the day on which they were sent.

You know, the people who-- the Jews who worked at the siding from which the trains left, the place called Umschlagplatz-- and I'm not sure I know the translation of the word 'umschlag,' but it was-- 'platz' means, of course, 'place,' 'starting place,' or something, 'loading place,' something like that, the siding which was in the ghetto from which the trains left.

The people who worked there-- there were some Jews who worked on loading the trains and then maintaining that little facility there, which had an infirmary and a waiting place, and so on, some buildings, they begin to notice that the trains came back the same day, meaning that, of course, they couldn't have gone very far.
And the trains were loaded absolutely to the gills.
And indications of bad things happening started to happen.
And then, of course, some people made it out of the camp.
The extermination camp where the Jewish-- where the Jews from Warsaw went was called Treblinka, T-R-E-B-L-I-N-K-A, which was just-- I don't know-- a couple hundred miles or less east of Warsaw.
And when they got there, as we later learned, some young healthy men would be picked to actually work temporarily in the camp.
And the rest of the people would be unloaded and taken to the showers and told to undress and gassed.
And this information filtered back.
And when it did, of course, then nobody was willing to go anymore.
So these selections worked in the following way.
In the morning, the Germans would cordon off some area, some-- a block or something like that of buildings.
And they always did it after the official workers had gone to work, so that the people who were left in those building were already, as we called them, wild, which meant illegal.
And these people were just free pickings for the Germans.
And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother and I would stay in those areas.
And in the apartment where we stayed at the time-- and I really don't remember it, it was a different apartment, but I don't remember how we got there-- we had a hiding place.
In the kitchen-- there was no refrigerator in that apartment, so in the kitchen, the walls were thick.
And under the kitchen window, there was a big cupboard where people used to keep things-- perishables so that they would be exposed colder, cooler kind of, kind of a cooler.
And it had doors and had two shelves.
And if there was a selection, my mother and I would lie on the shelves all day.
The door would be closed.
And a big, heavy kitchen table would be pushed against under the window, so that you couldn't see even that there was a cupboard there.
There were two other people in that apartment.
No, one other person.
I'm sorry.
A younger woman who had another hiding place where she could lock herself in.
So she would lock us up and then run over to her hiding place.
Her name was, I think, Helen.
The last name Yellin, Y-E-L-L-I-N.
You know, I don't know.
But it is possible, because she was educated in a Polish university.
And she had already sent her daughter out of the ghetto.
And I remember that she went out of the ghetto.
I know that her husband didn't make it.
But she may have made it.
You know, you-- you lost contact with people and you never found it again.
Some relatives never found each other after the war.
But anyway, I want to tell you a story that happened one time.
Usually by like 9 o'clock or 10 o'clock if nothing happened, that was it for the day.
We were safe.
But that particular day, they must have started late.
And we felt already safe, so we went about some little business in-- in the apartment.
And this woman, this Mrs.
Yellin, she did some laundry.
And she was hanging things out on a balcony that the apartment had.
She comes back in, white as a sheet.
And she said, they saw me.
There is a selection and they saw me.
She said, I want to hide you, and I'm lost, because they had seen me already.
And there was not even time to hide, because the bang on the door immediately.
I remember we were on the third floor, but this was a young Ukrainian soldier and he ran up as soon as he saw her and pushed in the door.
And he found us.
And she was-- she was a good-looking young woman.
And she started to beg him.
She says, I am so young.
I don't want to die yet.
Save me, please, you know.
And he said, all right.
And not only did he not take us, but he stood in the hall on that stairway.
And when the other soldiers came, he said, don't bother going in this apartment.
I was there already.
There's nobody there.
So we were saved.
But we could see-- you know, we went to the window and we looked through the-- the curtains.
And we could see them rounding up other people.
And this same young man dragged an old man with a beard out of an apartment.
And the man was resisting.
And he kicked him to death right in the yard, the same man who saved our lives.
So-- Why do you think he did that?
Well, what I-- what I learned from that is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you have the-- the power over the life or deaths of other people, it is both brutalizing and makes you very capricious.
So you could see events like this over and over.
The same person who would save one person's life and kill another person.
It gives you that sense of power that you can do whatever you want.
I mean, if you're going to kill everybody, then you don't have the sense of power.
You have the sense of power if you can grant life as well as deny it.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that seems to be how human nature often reacts to such-- I've seen such situations over and over.
It was not the only such situation.
Well, you know, the selections went on for about two or three months like that, in a-- in a kind of organized manner, but block by block.
Well--
Just about, yes.
Constantly frightened, very depressed, with a sense of hopelessness, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with very little to distract you from that.
And you looked-- on a day where you-- where you were safe, you looked for some distractions.
You tried to read something, to think about other things.
By this time, shortage of food, of course, so you're-- you're hungry a lot of the time, so you think a lot about food.
You think about the lack of body comforts.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You think about friends, some of whom I never-- most of whom I never saw again.

It was a very difficult three months, really extremely difficult, culminated in a big action, which was, by the way, described in a-- in a novel about the ghetto by Leon Uris called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was an action in which all the Jews of the ghetto were forced to go into a small section of the ghetto.
By this time, of course, probably about 2/3 of the population of the ghetto had been transported and exterminated.
And we were there for two or three days.
And out of that little section, only people with papers, official papers, were allowed to come out.
So for those few days, of course, they searched the entire perimeter, where we were not allowed to be.
And after that, they exterminated whoever stayed in that section who had not been able to come out.
So they felt it was a more efficient way of handling things.
And my mother and I managed to get out because of being friends with this Mr.
Rowinski, who in turn was sort of friends with Schultz, the owner, at the time, of that factory.
And they gave us some papers and allowed us to march out with the contingent from that factory.
And my father was with us, yes.
So we marched out.
And by this point-- at that point, the only places where you were allowed to live was in the buildings adjacent to the factories.
So now you didn't have one ghetto, but you had several tiny little ghettos.
And you were never allowed in the street, and you were-- except when you marched out with the whole group to work from one building to the next.
But, of course, my mother and I did not have work at that factory, so we were, again, wild in that building next to the-- to the factory.
And we could see that there was no good end for us all to this.
So in that factory, there were also Polish people working who came into the ghetto.
And my father was acquainted with them and negotiated with one gentleman by the name of Mr.
Rozwadowski, R-O-Z-W-A-D-O-W-S-K-I, who had two aunts living in a little town near Warsaw.
Very nice people, all of them.
And his aunts needed a little income, but they didn't want to fleece anybody.
They did want to help.

So my mother and I-- oh, by the way, after this big action in Mila Street, when we came back, I think there was only one or two searches of the surrounding buildings, because nobody was supposed to be there during the day.
Everybody was supposed to be at work.
And we had there also a hiding place where we spent the days.
But there were no big actions for a little while.
Whoever had survived the ghetto was-- more or less the ghettos really now, the little enclaves were more or less quiet.
And so my father arranged for us to come out.
And I don't remember why.
My mother and I came out first.
I think he wanted to sort of test the waters.
And it was-- my mother was a blonde.
I had sort of light brown hair.
And as I told you, my mother and I did not look Jewish.
We spoke-- I spoke perfect Polish, of course.
My mother spoke Polish with a Russian accent.
My father spoke Polish with a somewhat Yiddish accent.
And so when he opened his mouth, you know, you could tell.
You could tell the way he looked.
And you could always check with men, because in Poland only Jews were circumcised, so they could always [INAUDIBLE].
It was always a little easier for women, so he wanted us to escape first.
And we came out one evening after work with a group of Poles.
And what a contrast, you know.
Here we are in-- in this tense, frightening situation.
We come out, sadly, among Poles.
We are not used to being among Poles.
We are only among Jews.
And these people are living relatively normal lives, you know.
They're walking in the street.
There are stores.
We took a-- we took a tram to the train.
We got on the train.
And people were just going home from work, because this was maybe like an hour out of Warsaw or so by train.
It wasn't exactly a suburb.
It was more of a little resort town.
I think you can think of Long Beach, Long Island or maybe the Catskills or something like that.
Konstancin, K-O-N-S-T-A-N-C-I-N.
We had-- we had false papers.
But you see, as I have told you before, false papers first of all differed in quality.
I don't think ours were very good.
And they were always dangerous, because they could ask me questions-- for example, I did not know enough about Catholic religion.
I did not know how to pray.
So they could find me out in-- in a minute.
It was just good in a kind of routine checking of papers.
And I think I told you my name already on the-- in the-- on the Polish papers, no?
You can tell me again.
Barbara [?
Wozinska. ?] OK.
And so we-- we traveled on this train and got to Konstancin, to a villa with several apartments in it.
And we-- my mother and I had a room.
And later on, in several months, my father joined us.
And then we built a hiding place even there.
Behind a kind of a pantry, there was a walk-in pantry.
And we made it into a closet pantry.

And behind the shelves-- the shelves swung out and there was a little-- a little hiding room where we could be, and where, when these people had visitors-- because they-- they introduced my mother to a few friends as a distant relative from the provinces.
But she too could be detected too easily.
I suspect that the people to whom they introduced her knew that she was Jewish and were just-- they were nice people and they didn't report us, you know, to anybody.
But for the most part, if anybody visited them, we were either in our room or in this hiding place.
Their name was Rozwadowski, the same name as this man.
This was-- this man was their nephew.
There were two sisters.
One was a widow.
One was an unmarried, half-blind-- this was one reason that they needed people, because she could not manage to run the house.
The sister-- the widow sister worked to make a living.
There were two young men, the sister's sons, who were students.
One wanted to become a priest and one was an engineering student in Warsaw.
And I would read to the half-blind sister.
My mother did the housekeeping, helping her.
She was a sweet, sweet lady, really.
We spent most of the time with her.
And any visitors, we were just hidden for the most part, you know, not to take a chance.
My father was never seen, because he was a dead giveaway for being a-- a Jew.
